title: Lesson 332
transcript: An additional little bit to add in here related to role-based access is the different types of role-based access. This is a concept or this is an item that's shown up fairly recently on the exam. So there's four types of RBAC that you need to know about. And essentially what these are about is to what degree has the organization deployed role-based access within their organization. So the first type of RBAC is known as non-RBAC, which means there literally is no role-based access. Everything's probably rule-based access. So non-RBAC simply means that there's no role-based access being used in the organization at all. Limited RBAC, and listen carefully to the definition of this, because it's a subtle point. Limited RBAC is where within specific applications you can create some roles. So for instance, let's say app one there is our finance system. Within the finance system, you could create a finance payroll clerk level one and a finance payroll clerk level two and a finance payroll manager. So you can create some roles within the finance application. But what do you think? Would those roles in the finance application determine a user's access in, say, the CRM application? No, so that's what limited RBAC means. There is some application-specific roles, roles that you create within a specific application that just define a user's access within that specific application. So that's limited RBAC. The next option is what's known as hybrid RBAC. And again, listen carefully here, because this is where there's a subtle definition again. Hybrid RBAC is where you have some limited RBAC. There's some applications that you can just create some roles within those applications. But the big difference is when you get up to hybrid RBAC, you can now create a role that would allow a user to get access to a number of different applications. So through a single role, you could give a user access to the payroll system and the CRM system and something else. So that's the idea of hybrid RBAC. It's a combination of... application specific roles and the addition of where we can now create a role that gives a user access to multiple different systems. And then as you can probably guess, full RBAC is where role-based access has been fully deployed throughout the entire organization. And through a role, you could give a user access to any system that you want throughout the entire environment. That's full RBAC. Role-based access has been implemented in every system throughout the entire organization. And that is an incredibly hard thing to do, if not almost impossible, especially in larger organizations with a lot of legacy applications and things like that. So typically most organizations are not gonna make it all the way to full RBAC. They'll be sitting somewhere between limited or hybrid RBAC.
